Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> As the station manager, Martin doesn't cover a beat, but does forward pitches about politics, current affairs, literature, music and the arts to producers. "Do some homework and show some familiarity with what we do," he says. "Save yourself a lot of time by asking me honestly -- Is this the kind of thing that ends up on your station/program? If you find out I am interested in your pitch, be gently persistent. If you show that you're intelligent and understand the position I'm in as a gatekeeper, I might even look forward to hearing from you." Avoid celebrity pitches, self-improvement info, the newest tech toy, corporate product promotion and self-promoting CEOs. "As a publicist, if you can be a reliable resource when I need you, I'm more likely to be responsive when you bring me a pitch," he says. Reach him by email.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Musiker is a co-host of "The Do List," a weekly rundown of Northern California arts and cultural events. His beat is general assignment, but emphasizes local and state politics, the economy, the wine industry, arts and culture. "Make pitches timely, short, and with a Northern California angle," he says. "Put phone numbers up top. Use email, not snail mail. No faxes. No phone follow-up." Avoid pitching national experts without any grounding in California as well as out-of-town book authors on tour. A pitch for a national conference in San Francisco on global warming and carbon taxes resulted in a story. "It had phone numbers, attendees and an issue important to local listeners, which directly impacted state policy and timeliness," he says. Contact by email.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Conway manages the newsroom at the ABC news-talk affiliate. "I deal with all beats, including health, politics, business, the arts and other topics," she says. "Radio is immediate and the news cycle changes so rapidly that even a day's delay is problematic. The best PR people know how to grab a story that's in the news and tie it in to whatever they're pitching the day it's happening, not the day or week after," she says. "Anything that refers to great visuals is a huge turnoff. It tells me the person who is pitching has no idea where I work. I need sound, not pictures, and I need people who can talk now and not next week. I find it incredibly frustrating when the main players aren't available for interviews the day a major study is released," she says.  Reach the newsroom at 415-954-8142. Terry's direct line is 415-954-8615.
Outlet Profile: EDITORIAL PROFILE KGO-AM "NewsTalk 810" is a news talk radio station that serves the San Francisco Bay Area.  The highly rated station provides locally produced programming.  Some KGO-AM hosts, such as Dr. Dean Edell, are nationally syndicated.  The various hosts discuss a wide variety of topics and take calls from listeners.  KGO-AM is located in the same building as sister station KGO-TV (ABC7).  KGO-AM is owned by Citadel Broadcasting, but is still affiliated with ABC. The direct line for the newsroom is 415-954-8142.   Send general news releases via fax, or pitch directly to individual producers.  E-mailing talk show hosts press releases is not recommended. You can follow KGO-AM on Twitter at http://twitter.com/kgoradio.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Gonzalez is the sales manager for this Spanish radio station. "We cover Ventura County," he says. "The Hispanic audience is our target audience." Both local and national news apply. "We attract the Mexican population because we report news from Mexico, like sports and guests," he says. They're different programs throughout the day. "Mid-day, we play more soft music, more oldies," he says. "In the morning and afternoon, its more upbeat music." Any news, product or otherwise must "appeal directly to the Hispanic community, be something that might have to do with immigration, for example." The sooner the better as far as lead-time goes. "If I was going to interview Julio Iglesias, for example, I'd like to know as soon as possible," he says. Contact him via email. Best time to call is in the morning from 8:30 to 9:30 a.m. PST.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> (Pron. "hih MEN ez.") Jimenez says, "I collect and voice, that is, speak, business news in one-minute intervals, every half-hour." Covers jobs, local companies, finance, M&A, taxes, regulation, scandals, mortgages, the cost of money, housing, economics, stocks, bonds and other issues relating to business. She advises PR pros to "think like a newsperson: interesting, quick and clear." Wants to hear about "anything that helps people survive financially--numbers of jobs, affordability of real estate, hiring, firing, crazy new products." Seeks guests and sources that are "experts in their field and generalists, not advertisements for their companies, and [people] who are up at 6 or 7 a.m. Don't call me if you're just trying to please your CEO," she says. "I also want tech experts. Tech is a difficult thing to interpret. It's the finance side of tech I miss; it needs to mean something to the general public. Also, there's nothing wrong with having a source available that's just willing to be a source of information." Says the best PR pros give her a heads-up before sending a pitch. Prefers phone or fax. Call between 9 a.m. and noon.

Contact Profile: <From Bulldog> Is Health Policy Correspondent. In order to be effective your pitch must meet two key criteria: 1) it must have a news hook, 2) it must address a national trend. ""I like to focus on economics, managed care, changes in the market,"" says Neighmond. "" We profile doctors, hospitals and companies within the context of a greater story, such as a merger,"" she adds. Events such as hospital closings, for example, are perfect fodder for the show because ""the locals are obviously interested, also the general public, because a hospital closing represents something, perhaps a trend, that is happening in the industry as a whole."" Neighmond goes on to say that ""we never do complete feature stories, so I might not use the information you pitch until a news hook exists. But a PR pro might hear from me months later following up on a pitch."" So, addressing a potential trend early could pay off when the item becomes news. Neighmond prefers email, short and to-the-point. If you must call, be sure to ask if she has time to talk before launching into your pitch. Lead time is one week, except for breaking news.
